matching_policy.minimum_basis: At least one observed signal and one supporting source reference.
matching_policy.rule: Do not match from keywords alone. Apply exclusions and selected Archetype/Domain context.
matching_policy.output: Report observation, affected glossary terms and coordinates, consequence, risk, confirmation path, and focused correction.

patterns.name: Goal or Objective Drift
patterns.definition: The evaluated surface solves, optimizes, or validates a materially different target from the governing Goal or Objectives.
observable_signals: Goal wording changes without user authorization
local tasks pass while key Objectives disappear
new acceptance criteria replace the original target
exclusions: User explicitly changed the Goal
the difference is a documented clarification preserving meaning
visible_effects: complete-looking result for the wrong problem
misleading success claims
consequences: Project value decreases despite implementation activity
drift_risks: later versions compound the wrong direction
how_to_confirm: compare User Request, Intent, Goal, Objectives, and delivered outcomes
identify the first stage containing the changed meaning
how_to_fix: restore the original Goal/Objective boundary or obtain explicit user amendment
rewrite only affected prompts, profiles, metrics, or outputs
wrong_fix_patterns: add more tests without restoring the Goal
rewrite the whole Project when a focused correction is possible

patterns.name: Intent Routing Drift
patterns.definition: The User Request is normalized into the wrong Intent, Archetype, or Domain.
observable_signals: one request purpose is omitted
different purposes are collapsed
Archetype describes subject area rather than delivery type
exclusions: user explicitly reprioritized the request
secondary Domain is clearly labeled
visible_effects: wrong evaluation frame
irrelevant outputs or metrics
consequences: subsequent stages remain internally consistent but irrelevant
how_to_confirm: reconstruct request clauses and map each to Intent, Archetype, and Domain
how_to_fix: split or correct the Intent and rerun downstream evaluation
wrong_fix_patterns: patch result details while preserving wrong routing

patterns.name: Archetype Mismatch
patterns.definition: The selected Archetype does not match the Project's primary Delivery.
observable_signals: research report evaluated as app
profile package judged mainly by runtime behavior
prompt use mistaken for Prompt Archetype
exclusions: Project intentionally changed primary Delivery with explicit authorization
visible_effects: wrong priorities and missing critical dimensions
drift_risks: future versions optimize the wrong artifact shape
how_to_confirm: inspect purpose and material Deliveries
compare specialized profiles and exclusions
how_to_fix: select the correct primary Archetype and rescore relevant coordinates
wrong_fix_patterns: add many Archetypes simultaneously to avoid choosing

patterns.name: Domain Mismatch
patterns.definition: The evaluation or build uses constraints, metrics, or evidence standards from the wrong subject Domain.
observable_signals: generic business metric used for science validity
software correctness substituted for educational quality
trading assumptions omitted
exclusions: cross-domain Project explicitly documents multiple subject areas
visible_effects: important constraints and risks are invisible
how_to_confirm: identify the subject ontology and irreversible actions
review Domain-specific evidence expectations
how_to_fix: correct Domain selection and add missing domain criteria
wrong_fix_patterns: change Archetype when the issue is subject area

patterns.name: ADOS Profile Drift
patterns.definition: A Project claims ADOS Profile alignment while changing or omitting applicable concept or Pillar meaning.
observable_signals: profile name remains but Glossary or core-file roles change
Pillars copied without applicability analysis
self-compliance report substitutes for material alignment
exclusions: Profile version intentionally changed and differences are documented
visible_effects: compliance becomes nominal
consequences: downstream agents interpret the Package inconsistently
how_to_confirm: list claimed Profile concepts and applicable Pillars
compare material behavior and authority surfaces
how_to_fix: repair concept alignment or narrow the compliance Claim
wrong_fix_patterns: add a boolean profile-compliance field

patterns.name: Semantic Surface Drift
patterns.definition: Human and machine surfaces representing the same concept disagree.
observable_signals: term exists in one registry but not another
definition, status, parent, or cardinality differs
schema accepts a meaning rejected by Glossary
exclusions: surface is explicitly historical or compatibility-only
visible_effects: different consumers receive different meaning
consequences: semantic correctness and evaluation repeatability decline
drift_risks: later versions cannot identify the authoritative meaning
how_to_confirm: join surfaces by stable term or object ID
compare active definitions and classifications
how_to_fix: choose one authority and synchronize dependent surfaces
wrong_fix_patterns: increase schema coverage without resolving meaning

real_examples.observation: Term registries and SKOS/export surfaces were not fully synchronized.
real_examples.lesson: More semantic files do not establish semantic consistency.

patterns.name: Schema Coverage Without Contract Strength
patterns.definition: Every JSON instance is nominally covered while schemas do not constrain meaningful content.
observable_signals: critical schema requires only version or container type
unrestricted top-level properties
malformed semantic records validate
exclusions: open extension object is explicitly bounded and core fields remain strict
visible_effects: 100 percent coverage with low defect detection
likely_causes: coverage target substitutes for contract design
how_to_confirm: compare required fields and constraints with baseline
run meaningful negative examples
how_to_fix: restore semantic constraints and explicit extension points
wrong_fix_patterns: bind all instances to one generic schema

real_examples.observation: Complete governance was reported while critical schemas remained permissive or shallow.
real_examples.lesson: Coverage and strength are separate coordinates.

patterns.name: False PASS
patterns.definition: A source reports PASS or no blockers despite material unresolved defects or checks that cannot detect them.
observable_signals: validator accepts representative severe defects
PASS is self-authored by the same logic that built the artifact
unsupported zero-findings claim
exclusions: PASS is clearly scoped to one bounded check
visible_effects: overconfidence and incorrect baseline selection
how_to_confirm: inspect check scope and negative sensitivity
recompute key Claims independently
how_to_fix: narrow the Claim and correct the check or evidence
wrong_fix_patterns: add more PASS records or perfect scores

real_examples.observation: Package-owned validators reported PASS while adversarial review found material blind spots.
real_examples.lesson: PASS is a Claim whose scope must be evaluated.

patterns.name: Proof Fixture Overgeneralization
patterns.definition: A narrow example is treated as proof of broader Project or Package quality.
observable_signals: same known fixture reused as universal proof
fixture tests behavior unrelated to major package risks
fixture success appears in overall verdict
exclusions: Claim is explicitly limited to the exercised behavior
visible_effects: stable demo conceals other degradation
how_to_confirm: map each test to exact Claims
use varied or held-out examples when generalization matters
how_to_fix: narrow the Claim
add a materially different example only when generalization is required
wrong_fix_patterns: add a second trivial fixture without defining distinct coverage

real_examples.observation: simple-calculator arithmetic tests passed but did not test glossary semantics, schema strength, evidence integrity, or version drift.
real_examples.lesson: The calculator was bounded evidence, not the root cause.

patterns.name: Evidence Assertion Without Observation
patterns.definition: A record labels a Claim PASS without describing the relevant target, criteria, method, or observed result.
observable_signals: status field is the main evidence
target file points to itself as proof
many gates cite one generic record
exclusions: the source explicitly defines the record as a summary and raw evidence is available
visible_effects: conclusions cannot be independently interpreted
drift_risks: stale assertion reused in later versions
how_to_confirm: inspect evidence scope and provenance
resolve supporting artifacts
how_to_fix: capture scoped QA Evidence or mark the Claim unconfirmed
wrong_fix_patterns: create more assertion-only rows

real_examples.observation: Many work-verification records chiefly asserted PASS and target paths without observation-bearing detail.
real_examples.lesson: Record count is not evidence quality.

patterns.name: Build Package to Agent Work Drift
patterns.definition: Visible agent work materially diverges from the Build Package without an explicit user-authorized correction.
observable_signals: required concept or check omitted
unplanned architecture selected
scope expanded during implementation
exclusions: agent documents a necessary correction to contradictory controls and user approves it
visible_effects: result no longer represents the intended build
consequences: control quality and execution quality become conflated
drift_risks: future corrections target the wrong source
how_to_confirm: map major Build Package obligations to visible work and decisions
how_to_fix: correct agent instructions or Build Package depending on where the defect originated
wrong_fix_patterns: repair only the final Package without addressing recurring work drift

patterns.name: Agent Work to Build Result Drift
patterns.definition: The delivered result does not match the work, checks, or claims recorded by the building agent.
observable_signals: claimed file absent
reported check not reproducible
result contains unmentioned changes
exclusions: result was intentionally rebuilt after the recorded work and new evidence is supplied
visible_effects: trace and artifact tell different stories
how_to_confirm: compare final artifact with work claims and artifact identities
how_to_fix: regenerate result or update work evidence honestly
wrong_fix_patterns: edit only the summary to match desired state

patterns.name: Baseline Quality Regression
patterns.definition: A newer version loses confirmed quality of an older version while being treated as the automatic improvement baseline.
observable_signals: no cross-version contract comparison
new structure rewarded despite weaker semantics
latest chosen without rationale
exclusions: loss is an explicit accepted trade-off for a changed Goal
visible_effects: future work starts from a weaker base
how_to_confirm: rank candidate baselines against next Goal and confirmed coordinates
how_to_fix: select the strongest baseline and reuse later ideas only after review
wrong_fix_patterns: start from latest because version is higher

real_examples.observation: v1.3 retained stronger verified semantic/schema contracts while later versions added useful but weaker structures.
real_examples.lesson: Baseline is a quality decision, not a version sort.

patterns.name: Version or Package Identity Drift
patterns.definition: Active package surfaces identify different versions, names, statuses, or authority paths.
observable_signals: root and machine metadata disagree
old version appears in active fields
repeated version suffix
exclusions: reference is explicitly historical or compatibility context
visible_effects: ambiguous artifact identity and wrong source selection
consequences: evidence and comparisons attach to wrong version
how_to_confirm: classify active, historical, example, and compatibility references
how_to_fix: repair active identity and preserve historical context explicitly
wrong_fix_patterns: replace all version strings without classification

real_examples.observation: Version replacement and stale references created active identity inconsistencies.
real_examples.lesson: Identity is a model, not a string-replacement task.

patterns.name: Metric Substitution
patterns.definition: A convenient count or score substitutes for the quality concept it was intended to approximate.
observable_signals: schema count treated as schema quality
test count treated as correctness
file count treated as completeness
total score hides degraded coordinate
exclusions: the metric is explicitly bounded and paired with semantic analysis
visible_effects: optimization targets the number rather than the Goal
consequences: quality appears to improve while core value declines
how_to_confirm: trace Metric to Coordinate and Goal
inspect what the evidence actually measures
how_to_fix: change or contextualize the Metric and expose coordinate detail
wrong_fix_patterns: add more count metrics

patterns.name: Over-Governance Drift
patterns.definition: Governance mechanisms become the main artifact and displace the Project's original evaluation or delivery Goal.
observable_signals: more workflow or ledger rules than Domain logic
auditor requires Projects to adopt its process
correction adds unrelated package governance
exclusions: the Project Goal is genuinely a governance or control system
visible_effects: complexity grows while product usefulness falls
consequences: agents optimize compliance surfaces rather than quality
drift_risks: future versions become harder to change
how_to_confirm: compare governance surface growth with Goal and user value
how_to_fix: remove mandatory mechanics not required for evaluation; keep concepts and schemas as guardrails
wrong_fix_patterns: respond by adding another mandatory ledger or validator

real_examples.observation: The earlier design over-centered ledgers, validators, hard gates, and execution process rather than glossary/RAG semantic evaluation.
real_examples.lesson: A defect-prevention mechanism can itself become Goal drift.

patterns.name: Terminology Drift
patterns.definition: Stable concept names or definitions change, broaden, narrow, or conflict without controlled adaptation.
observable_signals: same term used for different concepts
new local definition
app-specific term used as universal Project concept
exclusions: a new term or definition is explicitly approved and migration is documented
visible_effects: different files and agents interpret the Project differently
consequences: drift findings and metrics become unreliable
how_to_confirm: retrieve glossary term records and compare source wording by concept
how_to_fix: adopt one definition, add mapping or subtype, and update references
wrong_fix_patterns: global text replacement without concept review

real_examples.observation: APP Requirements required review and generalization to Project Requirements because App is only one Archetype.
real_examples.lesson: Terminology migration must follow concept applicability.

patterns.name: Scope Drift
patterns.definition: Work or Delivery adds, removes, or changes material scope outside the governing request and Intent.
observable_signals: unrequested components dominate
required surfaces removed
corrective build changes unrelated terms or files
exclusions: user explicitly authorizes scope change
visible_effects: time and quality are spent on the wrong surface
how_to_confirm: compare requested and delivered scope; classify intentional changes
how_to_fix: remove or authorize expansion and focus correction on affected surfaces
wrong_fix_patterns: hide expansion under quality-improvement wording

patterns.name: App Requirement Overgeneralization
patterns.definition: An obligation designed for App Projects is applied as a universal Project Requirement.
observable_signals: APP-prefixed doctrine copied into non-App profiles
runnable UI or app scaffolding required for research/profile work
exclusions: the obligation is shown to be Archetype-independent and is generalized explicitly
visible_effects: irrelevant files, tests, or governance appear
likely_causes: App treated as synonym for Project
how_to_confirm: review Requirement purpose and all supported Archetypes
how_to_fix: generalize to Project Requirement or retain as App subtype
wrong_fix_patterns: rename APP to Project without checking substantive applicability
